interrogator_dossier:
# HEICO Corporation (HEI) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8 · Transcripts Q2 FY25 (2025-05-28) → Q2 FY26 (2026-05-28), six consecutive quarters · Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM (2026-04-30)*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across six quarters the HEICO story did not pivot — it *intensified along the same axis*. The core narrative is unwavering: a decentralized, cash-funded, serial-acquisition compounder in the aerospace aftermarket (PMA parts / DER repairs) plus a specialty defense-electronics arm, run by the founding Mendelson family with a near-religious culture of "do the right thing" and "real cash, not artificial GAAP."

- **Q2 FY25 (2025-05-28):** Frame set — "record results," FSG +19% sales / +24% OI on "14% organic growth," Wencor "the gift that keeps on giving," and a *new* macro overlay introduced: "the current administration's anticipated pro-business direction aligns well with our long-term goals." Defense/space named as the priority lanes. Net-debt/EBITDA already improving (2.06x → 1.86x).
- **Q3 FY25 (2025-08-26):** Same drumbeat, "robust double-digit organic growth," leverage to 1.9x even after $630M of YTD acquisitions; Gables ("third-largest acquisition in HEICO's history") signed. The cash-self-funding model is explicitly canonized: "for over 36 years… fund our organic growth with cash generated by operations and not incur debt to grow organically."
- **Q4 FY25 (2025-12-19):** Emotional inflection — the death of founder/Chairman Larry Mendelson. The brothers re-anchor the story to *legacy and culture* ("his succession plan… worked perfectly"). Operationally the strongest quarter yet: FSG OI +30%, leverage down to 1.60x, two more deals (incl. Ethos) signed to close in Q1 cal-26.
- **Q1 FY26 (2026-02-26):** A "secret sauce" monologue ("obedience to the unenforceable… we've never had a one-time unusual charge"), Golden Dome / $1.5T defense-budget tailwind introduced as a *named multi-year driver*. FSG OI +21%, ETG organic decelerates to +6% (space shipments timing).
- **Q2 FY26 (2026-05-28):** The blow-out — consolidated net income **+49%**, sales **+25%**, EBITDA +37%, ETG OI **+56%** / FSG OI +31%; ETG organic re-accelerates to +17%, FSG organic to +19%. Margin expansion called out (op margin ~26%). Management now says HEICO is "firing on all engines."

**Drivers introduced and *kept*:** decentralized M&A, Wencor cross-sell, defense/space/Golden Dome, PMA-vs-OEM cost arbitrage (amplified by the fuel-price spike — "new customers approach HEICO about PMA parts… the simple one-word answer is yes"). **Nothing of consequence was quietly dropped** — a rare and bullish narrative-integrity signal.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter(s) | Hard-number check | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record results," double-digit organic growth | every Q | Rev 2.97B→3.86B→4.49B (FY23→25), +16.3% YoY; 3-yr CAGR +26.6%; Q2 FY26 sales +25% | **MATCHES** — corroborated, not puffery |
| EPS compounding | every Q | EPS 2.91→3.67→4.90 (FY23→25), +33.5% YoY; TTM diluted EPS **5.60**; Q2 FY26 EPS $1.66 (+48% YoY) | **RICHER than headline** — TTM already 5.60 vs 4.90 FY |
| "Fund organic growth with cash, not debt" | Q3 FY25 | TTM FCF **926M**, FCF margin 19.2%, +40.3% YoY; net-debt/EBITDA fell 2.06x→1.60x→1.74x *while* buying 5+ companies | **MATCHES** — genuine cash engine, deleverages through M&A |
| Margin strength / SG&A leverage | Q2 FY26 | Gross margin 39.8% but flagged *contracting*; net margin 15.4%; op margin ~26% and rising on mix+volume | **MOSTLY MATCHES** — op leverage real; gross-margin contraction is the honest caveat |
| Accretive, disciplined M&A | every Q | ROIC only **10.0%**, ROE 14.0% — *below* the long-run average and below TDG's 12–15%; goodwill/amortization heavy | **DIVERGES** — the acquisition flywheel dilutes near-term returns on capital; growth is partly *bought* |
| Beat consistency | implied | Beat **7/7 quarters (100%)**; EPS YoY +33.5%, latest-Q +48% | **MATCHES** — flawless delivery |

**Where the story is richer than the tape:** TTM EPS (5.60) and FCF (926M) outrun the stale FY-annual figures — the business is accelerating, not decelerating. **Where it is poorer:** ROIC at 10% and gross margin "contracting" reveal that a chunk of the spectacular top-line is *M&A-purchased* growth carrying goodwill drag; the per-share compounding is real but the *capital efficiency* is currently sub-peer.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING (and arguably the most confident in the six-quarter set at Q2 FY26).**

- Q2 FY25: "filled with deep optimism" (Victor); "hit the ball out of the park" (Eric).
- Q3 FY25: "humbled… another outstanding quarter"; cash "exceptionally strong."
- Q4 FY25: grief-tempered but operationally triumphant — "even more proud," leverage at a multi-year low.
- Q1 FY26: aspirational/philosophical ("secret sauce," "designed for long-term… sustainable earnings increases"); confident enough to wave off accounting noise.
- Q2 FY26: peak swagger — "**firing on all engines**," "business is very strong virtually across the board," "real cash" repeated for emphasis; even jokes ("over the moon," puns for the late analyst Rob Stallard).

The language is *consistently offensive* (growth/investment), never defensive. No shift to cost-cutting/efficiency framing — when efficiency is mentioned (SG&A leverage) it is presented as a *byproduct of growth*, not a lever pulled under duress. Guidance specificity is deliberately *low* (HEICO famously does not give formal guidance), but qualitative forward statements grew more emphatic, not hedged.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

HEICO gives no formal numeric guidance — a long-standing, principled stance ("we're not into… comparing ourselves to others hoping to get a higher multiple"). So credibility is judged on *qualitative forward statements vs subsequent results*:

- Q2 FY25 promised continued strength across all product lines → Q3/Q4 FY25 delivered FSG OI +29%/+30%. **Delivered.**
- Q4 FY25 said Ethos + a second deal "should close in Q1 cal-26" → both closed; CalRamic added June-2026. **Delivered.**
- Q1 FY26 framed Golden Dome/defense as a multi-year backlog tailwind → Q2 FY26 defense pull-forward ($15–20M) and record orders/backlog. **Delivered/tracking.**
- Forward-looking margin: Carlos *raised* his FSG margin range to "24–26%" and ETG to "22–24%" in Q2 FY26 — an upward revision, the opposite of sandbag-then-miss.

**Beat rate 7/7 (100%)** corroborates. The only "honest negatives" volunteered (ETG space shipment timing in Q1, stock-comp amortization acceleration, gross-margin contraction, the LCP cash-flow distributions that are "net cash neutral") are *disclosed proactively* — a credibility-positive pattern. **Reliability: HIGH.**

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **Centralized supply chain / margin unlock (Kristine Liwag, Q3 FY25):** Analysts repeatedly probe whether HEICO could "capture more margin" by centralizing procurement. Management firmly declines ("not in the next 3–5 years… not worth disrupting our happy customer base"). *Not a deflection — a strategic boundary, consistently held.*
- **Pricing power / contract roll-offs (Liwag, Q3 FY25):** Analysts push on raising PMA prices given OEM inflation; Eric explicitly *refuses* the "profit grab" ("that's not the HEICO way… I would not model that"). Bullish on integrity, bearish for anyone modeling a margin step-change.
- **Class A vs common stock collapse (Gautam Khanna, Q4 FY25):** Recurring governance question; management says "status quo… Class A is a screaming value," no plan to collapse. **A live, unresolved governance/special-sit lever that management itself flags as undervalued.**
- **China pull-forward / tariff destocking (Q2 FY25, Q4 FY25):** Analysts worried about pre-buying; management denied any region-specific distortion ("strength in all areas"). Q2 FY26 then *did* disclose a $15–20M defense pull-forward — a small, honestly-quantified version of the risk analysts feared.
- **What analysts under-press:** the ROIC-dilution / goodwill question and the valuation (54x FCF) — the bull thesis is so consensus that the harder questions (capital efficiency, multiple sustainability) get little airtime.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **Flawless delivery:** 7/7 beats, EPS +33.5% YoY, TTM EPS 5.60 > FY 4.90 (accelerating).
🟢 **Deleveraging through M&A:** net-debt/EBITDA 2.06x → 1.60x while spending >$1B on deals; $2.2B credit facility for dry powder.
🟢 **Real cash:** TTM FCF 926M, 19% FCF margin, "130% of net income" cash conversion (Q3 FY25); LCP distributions disclosed as non-cash.
🟢 **Narrative integrity:** no dropped drivers, no one-time charges in 36 years (a verifiable, falsifiable claim management stakes its reputation on).
🟢 **Insider/director conviction:** directors bought **Class A** ("screaming value") with their retainer — a tell.
🔴 **Sub-peer ROIC (10.0%)** below HEICO's own history and TDG's 12–15% — growth is partly *purchased*, goodwill-heavy.
🔴 **Gross margin contracting** (39.8%, flagged) — amortization from the deal cadence (Gables, Exxelia-style) dampens reported OI margin.
🔴 **Valuation:** Price 331.61 vs Fair Value 313.1 (MoS **−6%**), P/FCF **54.2x**, ~61x P/E; *every* per-method MoS deeply negative (DCF −100%, EPV −100%, Graham −56%). The quality is undisputed; the *price* is the whole risk.
🔴 **Open-market insider selling** dominates by share count (13 sells / 230,496 sh vs 21 buys / 17,613 sh) — buys are conviction-flavored but sellers are voting with size.
🔴 **Key-man/family control:** dual-class, family co-CEOs; founder Larry just died — succession is "embedded" per management, but it is a genuine concentration risk.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology escalation:** "deep optimism" (Q2 FY25) → "firing on all engines… virtually across the board" (Q2 FY26). The qualifier "virtually" is the only hedge in an otherwise unqualified bull narrative.
- **Fuel-price spike repurposed as a tailwind:** in Q2 FY26 management turns the oil/Iran-war shock into a *demand driver* — high fuel → airlines seek HEICO's cheaper PMA parts ("new customers… yes, 100%"). A genuinely counter-cyclical wrinkle to the thesis.
- **Margin-range *raised*, not lowered** (Carlos, Q2 FY26): ETG 22–24%, FSG 24–26% — a quiet upward reset of the structural margin floor.
- **Capital allocation unchanged but scaled:** dividend kept token ($0.12 semiannual, 95th consecutive) — they hoard cash for M&A, not payouts; the shareholder base is total-return/quality, not yield. No buyback emphasis.
- **Class A "screaming value" repeated** — management is effectively telling the market the A-shares (and a future class-collapse) are an embedded option. The *only* identifiable discrete corporate-action lever in the story, and it is non-dated and entirely at the family's discretion.
- **No new voices / no strategy drift** post-Larry: Eric, Victor, Carlos are the same three; the death changed the *tone* (legacy framing) but not a single operational policy.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STRENGTHENING.** Every operating and cash metric inflected *up* over the six quarters, delivery is 7/7, leverage fell while M&A accelerated, and the narrative never broke discipline. This is a genuine, corroborated serial-compounder operating at the top of its game.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *this is a quality-at-any-price problem, not a quality problem.* HEICO is a 5-star business trading at a 0-star margin of safety — 54x FCF / ~61x P/E with a **−6% margin of safety** and *every* intrinsic method screaming overvaluation, into a stock already up ~18% in a month and into rising analyst PTs. There is **no discrete, dated, asymmetric catalyst** — the Q2 FY26 blow-out already fired and re-rated the stock; the M&A flywheel is continuous (good for a multi-year hold, useless as a catalyst trade); Golden Dome/defense is a multi-year soft tailwind; the only special-sit lever (Class A collapse) is undated and family-controlled. The director is being asked to pay a full toll-booth price for compounding that is real but already in the tape. Size as a *quality compounder on weakness*, never as an event/catalyst position.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 5 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING

bull_thesis: HEICO is an operationally pristine aerospace-aftermarket compounder firing on two engines into a genuine multi-year tailwind. The financials corroborate the narrative on every axis: revenue +16.3% YoY (3-yr CAGR +26.6%), TTM diluted EPS $5.60 with latest-Q EPS +48%, beat 7/7 quarters, TTM FCF $926M at a 19.2% FCF margin, and net-debt/EBITDA falling 2.06x->1.60x even after ~$630M of self-funded deals. FQ2'26 (2026-05-28) was a blowout: consolidated NI +49%, FSG organic +19%, ETG organic +17%, with management 'firing on all engines... business is very strong virtually across the board.' The moat is real and durable: FAA-PMA approvals + DER repair authority on flight-critical, long-life platforms (regulatory-license + intangible + switching-cost). The franchise is anti-fragile to the obvious tails -- OEM price inflation and defense cost-pressure make HEICO's 50-70%-discount PMA parts MORE attractive; Eric (FQ2'26) confirms fuel-price spikes are bringing in NEW PMA customers and reverse-engineering requests. Succession through Larry Mendelson's death (late-2025) was pre-built and results did not blink. For an owner willing to pay for durability and a decade-long reinvestment runway, this is a wide-moat quality compounder you hold through the cycle. bear_thesis: The bear case is entirely about PRICE, and it is a strong one. moderator_conclusion: PASS (C), conviction 2. This is the cleanest possible 'great company, wrong price, wrong regime' case. Verdict C / conviction 2: own-the-business holders of an existing position are fine, but for NEW capital allocation under this regime and at this price, the risk/reward is unattractive (~45% to a peer-de-rate vs ~0 upside-to-fair). If the thesis is wanted, the WATCHLIST trigger would be a growth-deceleration or air-traffic-driven flush that resets the multiple toward the low-$200s SoP -- buy the quality on the washout, not at the high.

# HEICO Corporation (HEI) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8 · Capital-committee dossier · transcripts FQ2'25 → FQ2'26 (six consecutive calls) cross-referenced against screener financials*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Across six consecutive calls the core story does not shift — it **compounds**. That is itself the signal: a company whose narrative is stable, specific, and serially over-delivered is rare. The arc widens in three deliberate ways.

**(a) From "Wencor integration" to "firing on all engines."** In FQ2'25 (2025-05-28) the marginal story is still the Wencor combination — *"the Wencor and Legacy HEICO operations continue to exceed our expectations… this was an excellent combination"* — and the proof point is 14% organic FSG growth on top of deals. By FQ2'26 (2026-05-28) Wencor is barely mentioned; the company is *"firing on all engines, business is very strong for us virtually across the board"* with FSG organic at **19%** and ETG organic at **17%**. The driver set has broadened from one transformative deal to a fully-firing two-engine machine.

**(b) Defense / space / Golden Dome introduced and amplified.** Defense is a recurring, escalating theme. FQ3'25 frames defense as *"an excellent opportunity, especially as the current administration prioritizes defense and cost efficiency."* By FQ1'26 (2026-02-26) it is explicit and dated to policy: management is asked about the *"$1.5 trillion budget"* and *"Golden Dome,"* and answers candidly — *"there's no Golden Dome department… we're down a layer in the supplier base… both are positives for us."* By FQ2'26 defense is *"now experiencing this in our defense sales, in our defense orders and our defense backlog… a multiyear tail for which we are very well prepared."* This is a genuine secular accelerant being layered onto the aftermarket base, but management is careful never to over-claim a single program.

**(c) M&A cadence is relentless and rising.** Five acquisitions in FY25 (Rosen, Gables — *"third-largest in HEICO's history"* — plus three more), then four more in 1H FY26 (Axillon fuel containment, Sherwood, Ethos, others), all funded by operating cash. The capital-allocation engine is the constant: *"a core tenet… is to fund our organic growth with cash generated by operations and not incurred debt"* (FQ3'25).

**Founder transition is the one genuinely new event.** Larry Mendelson (Chairman/CEO, 36 years) **died** between FQ3'25 and FQ4'25; the FQ4'25 (2025-12-19) and FQ1'26 calls open with extended eulogies, and Victor & Eric are now Co-Chairmen/Co-CEOs. The succession was pre-built (*"that was his succession plan… it worked perfectly"*) and the results did not blink — but a director must weigh that the founder-anchor is gone.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | Financials say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record results… consolidated net income +49%" | FQ2'26 | FY25 NI $690M vs FY24 $514M (+34%); latest-Q EPS YoY **+48%**; TTM diluted EPS $5.60 | **Corroborated** — the +49% Q is real and the TTM EPS confirms acceleration, not a one-off |
| "Strong organic growth" (13–19% FSG) | all 6 | Revenue +16.3% YoY, **3-yr CAGR +26.6%**; organic is a large share of that | **Corroborated** — organic genuinely double-digit, deals additive on top |
| "Real cash, not artificial GAAP metrics" | FQ4'25/FQ1'26 | FCF +40.3% YoY, 3-yr CAGR +25.5%; **TTM FCF $926M**; FCF margin 19.2% | **Strongly corroborated** — cash conversion is the spine of the model and the numbers back the rhetoric |
| "We delever quickly… funded by operations" | FQ3'25 | Net debt/EBITDA fell 2.06x → 1.60x (Oct-25) even after $630M of deals; recomputed **net debt $2.38B** | **Corroborated with nuance** — leverage is modest and self-amortizing, but this is NET DEBT, not net cash; the balance sheet is clean, not pristine |
| "Margin expansion… we're busting at the seams" | FQ2'26 | Net margin 15.4%, FCF margin 19.2%; gross margin 39.8% flagged **contracting**; margin **1.45σ above historical peak** | **Mixed** — operating leverage is real, but the screener flags gross margin contraction and margins sitting 1.45σ rich; the upside is being earned at a cyclical high-water mark |
| Beat cadence ("record" every quarter) | all 6 | **Beat 7/7 (100%)**; EPS +33.5% YoY, EPS 5yr CAGR +22% | **Corroborated** — flawless recent delivery |
| ROIC / returns quality | — | **ROIC 10.0%, ROE 14.0%** | **Tension** — for a "compounder" the reported ROIC is only ~10%, depressed by acquisition goodwill/intangibles; the *incremental* economics are far better than the blended return suggests, but the headline return is unremarkable for the multiple paid |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers:** the "compounder at any scale" framing implies a premium return profile, yet blended ROIC is ~10% — the multiple (P/FCF ~55x) is paying for *durability and reinvestment runway*, not for an exceptional spot return. **Where it is poorer than the trajectory justifies:** nowhere material — this is the unusual case where the financials are at least as strong as the rhetoric. The forensic risk is not credibility; it is **price**.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Rising / Stable-high.** Confidence is consistently high and, if anything, climbs into FQ2'26.

- FQ2'25: *"filled with deep optimism… uniquely positioned"* — Eric: *"our team members literally hit the ball out of the park."*
- FQ3'25: *"robust double-digit organic growth… we remain very optimistic."*
- FQ4'25: through Larry's death — *"more optimistic than ever about this company's prospects."*
- FQ1'26: a notably *philosophical* call — Eric volunteers the "secret sauce" monologue (*"obedience to the unenforceable became our DNA"*), a confident, almost legacy-defining tone.
- FQ2'26: peak confidence — *"firing on all engines… HEICO has always been and will always be where the industry goes and where it grows."*

**No defensive pivot.** The language stays **offensive** (growth, investment, market-share gains) throughout. The only defensive notes are honest, unsolicited macro caveats — the China tariff pause (FQ2'25), the *"current just war"* / Middle East softness and a ~$15–20M defense pull-forward (FQ2'26). Management *volunteers* the negatives rather than burying them, which raises rather than lowers credibility. Guidance specificity actually **increases** late: Carlos gives a concrete FSG margin range (*"24% to 26%"*) and ETG range in FQ2'26 — more numeric guidance than earlier calls.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** HEICO is famously light on formal numeric guidance (no annual EPS guide), so the test is *qualitative promises kept*. On that basis the record is excellent:

- "Accretive within the first/year following acquisition" (Rosen, Gables) — pattern historically delivered; deals visibly additive to segment records.
- Carlos's FQ3'25 hedge that Gables amortization *"will dampen the OI margin a little"* but *"not as pronounced as Exxelia"* — followed by FQ2'26 record ETG margins (+56% operating income), i.e. the conservative framing under-promised.
- The LCP-distribution disclosure (FQ1'26): management flagged a ~$73M operating-cash drag for the remainder of FY26 but explained it is *"net cash neutral"* (funded by corporate-owned life insurance, an investing inflow) — proactive, granular, non-promotional disclosure of a metric-distorting item.

**Sandbagging pattern:** mild and structural — they consistently frame margin/accretion conservatively, then beat. Combined with **7/7 beats** in the financials, this is a sandbag-and-beat profile, not a stretch-and-miss one. **Score: HIGH.**

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts probe four recurring areas; management answers most directly, deflects two for competitive reasons:

1. **Centralizing the supply chain for margin** (Liwag, FQ3'25): pushed on how much margin a centralized purchasing function could capture. Carlos **declines to quantify** (*"I haven't done a study to give you a precise number"*) and argues the decentralized model is worth the cost. Honest, but a recurring un-modeled margin lever left vague.
- **2. PMA pricing power / contract roll-offs** (Liwag, FQ3'25): analysts see latent pricing (parts at 50–70% OEM discount); Eric explicitly **refuses the profit-grab** (*"that's really not the HEICO way… I would not model that kind of activity"*). A deliberate decision to leave near-term margin on the table — bullish for durability, a drag on the bear's "margins are peaking" thesis.
3. **Specific engine/PMA program penetration & defense end-market splits** (Khanna, Raffetto): repeatedly **deflected** *"for competitive reasons"* / *"we did not break that out publicly."* Standard for HEICO; not evasive about results, only about competitive granularity.
4. **The dual-class A/common structure** (Khanna, FQ4'25): Victor calls Class A *"a screaming value"* that *"a rational market will recognize"* — a soft governance topic management treats as status-quo.

No instance in the window where a deflected topic later became bad news. Boeing MAX destocking (Ciarmoli, FQ3'25) was flagged as a risk and management's *"little bit"* answer has so far proven accurate.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Valuation, not operations.** Every methodological margin-of-safety is deeply negative (DCF −100%, EPV −100%, OwnerErn −100%, IV15 −100%, Graham −58%); P/FCF **54.8x**, P/E ~62x. The market has fully priced the quality. *(Screener; corroborated by external P/E ~61.6x vs industry ~40x.)*
🔴 **Margins 1.45σ above historical peak** and **gross margin flagged contracting** — incremental upside is being booked at a cyclical/structural high.
🔴 **Insider open-market signal is negative:** 21 buys (17,613 sh) vs 13 sells (**230,496 sh**) — sells dwarf buys ~13:1 by share count. Family/insiders are net sellers into strength.
🔴 **Founder-anchor gone** (Larry Mendelson died, late 2025) — succession pre-built, but the 36-year culture-keeper is no longer a backstop.
🔴 **ROIC ~10% / Piotroski 5/9** — blended returns and the F-score are merely average, diluted by acquisition intangibles.

🟢 **Financials corroborate the narrative on every major axis** — organic growth, FCF conversion, beat cadence, self-funded deleveraging. Rare alignment.
🟢 **FAA-PMA + DER repair franchise** is a genuine regulatory/intangible moat with switching costs (qualified parts on flight-critical platforms).
🟢 **Multi-engine secular tailwind** — aftermarket supercycle + defense/space budget expansion + Golden Dome layer, with HEICO's low-cost PMA positioned exactly where budget-cost-pressure points.
🟢 **Honest, proactive disclosure** — volunteers macro softness, the LCP cash-flow distortion, and refuses promotional moves (no profit-grab, no multiple-chasing).

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology shift toward permanence.** Early calls sell a *quarter*; FQ1'26–FQ2'26 sell an *institution* ("do the right thing," "obedience to the unenforceable," "where the industry goes and where it grows"). Post-founder-death, management is consciously codifying the culture as the durable asset — a tell that the *people* risk is top-of-mind even as results stay clean.
- **The "no one-time charges in 36 years" boast** (FQ1'26) is a pointed, unprompted swipe at peers who *"erase upcycle earnings after a black swan."* It is a credibility flex aimed squarely at the bear case that aerospace earnings are cyclical — and it is backed by the clean trajectory.
- **Class A "screaming value"** language (FQ4'25) is a quiet signal that *even management thinks part of its own equity is mispriced* — but that is the low-vote A shares vs the common, an intra-structure arb, not a statement that the enterprise multiple is cheap.
- **CapEx held at ~1.5–1.6% of revenue** through a record-demand surge — asset-light, cash-generative model intact; they are not chasing growth with capital intensity.
- **Defense framing as "speed, not cost-plus"** (FQ4'25): Victor positions HEICO as structurally advantaged for a DOGE-style cost/speed defense regime — a subtle re-rating argument that the defense mix is *higher-quality* than cost-plus primes.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STRENGTHENING.** The earnings base is widening (two engines firing, defense backlog building, M&A compounding), cash conversion is excellent, leverage is falling, and the narrative is corroborated by the financials on every material axis. This is an operationally pristine compounder accelerating into a genuine secular tailwind.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** this is a **price problem, not a quality problem.** At P/FCF ~55x / P/E ~62x with *every* intrinsic-value method showing a deeply negative margin of safety, the stock already capitalizes flawless multi-year execution. The forensic edge here is **negative** — there is no expectations gap to arbitrage; consensus and the tape already love it (stock +7% vs 200d, 75% up the 52-wk range, analyst targets being chased upward to $390–410). The "catalyst" (defense/aftermarket supercycle) is already firing in the price, not a hidden re-rate. Insiders are net sellers into the strength. Size for a *quality-compounder-at-a-rich-multiple* (a hold/own-the-business decision), **not** for a mispriced special-situation re-rate.

**Economic moat:** intangible-asset + regulatory-license (FAA-PMA approvals, DER repair authority) layered with switching-costs (qualified parts on flight-critical, long-life platforms). Judged on the EVIDENCE: gross margin is **contracting** (39.8%, flagged) and blended **ROIC is ~10%** (diluted by acquisition intangibles) — so the *durable, high-return aftermarket core* is wide, but the *blended* return profile is being held flat-to-down by the acquisitive structure and a margin sitting 1.45σ above its own peak. Net read: **WIDE moat, STABLE trend** — the franchise is durable and the secular tailwind is real, but it is not visibly *widening* on the reported returns; the premium is for durability, not for an accelerating return on capital.

**Secular force / terminal value:** No structural terminal threat. The aftermarket-PMA model is *anti-fragile* to the obvious tails — higher OEM prices and budget cost-pressure make HEICO's discount parts *more* attractive (Eric, FQ2'26: fuel-price spikes are bringing *new* PMA customers). The risks are **cyclical** (a commercial-aerospace/air-traffic downturn, a defense-budget reversal), not secular impairment of the franchise. Cheapness is irrelevant here because the stock is **not cheap** — this is a quality name at a fear-free, fully-priced multiple, the inverse of a value trap and the inverse of a fear-print.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 5 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING | MOAT: WIDE | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: none

bull_thesis: HEICO is a best-in-class quality compounder firing on every cylinder, and the live Q2-FY26 print (reported 2026-05-27) confirms the flywheel is ACCELERATING, not maturing: EPS $1.66 vs $1.33 consensus (+25% beat, now 8/8 quarters), net income +49%, sales +25%, Flight Support Group (FSG) +19% organic with operating margin EXPANDING to 26.2% (from 24.1%) and Electronic Technologies Group (ETG) to 26.5%. Conservative-accounting culture (deliberate reserving, 'never had a one-time unusual charge,' OCF 130% of NI) understates economic earnings. Rev 3-yr CAGR +26.6%, EPS CAGR +24.3%, TTM FCF $926M. The bull bet: pay a premium for a verified 20%+ compounder with no secular threat and grow into the multiple; analysts uniformly raised targets to a ~$386 average post-Q2.
bear_thesis: Not a bad business — a fully-priced one. HEI trades at ~60x trailing / ~63x forward P/E and P/FCF 55x: a ~60% premium to its closest premium-aftermarket comp TransDigm (~37-38x P/E, verified June 2026) and ~22% above HEICO's own 10-year average P/E of ~51x. The only scheduled events are routine quarterly prints (next ~65 days out) and semiannual dividend declarations, neither of which forces a re-rating; they are momentum check-ins, not consensus-shattering catalysts. The load-bearing catalyst (the Q2-FY26 blowout) already fired on 2026-05-27 and is in the price. With no dated binding event and no asymmetry, the name fails the catalyst-density test.
valley_of_death: No balance-sheet valley — net-debt/EBITDA 1.74x, Altman-Z 8.4, OCF 130% of NI, no maturity wall, no cash-burn hump. The near-term risk is purely de-rating, not solvency. moderator_conclusion: PASS (C), conviction 2. HEICO is an A+ business at a D- price, evaluated through a regime that explicitly penalizes exactly this profile. # HEICO Corporation (HEI) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator's report to the investment committee. Transcripts: Q2-FY25 (2025-05-28) through Q1-FY26 (2026-02-26), four consecutive calls, cross-referenced against the screener's financial trajectory and live Q2-FY26 results (reported 2026-05-27).*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The HEICO story across these four calls is striking for what it does **not** do: it does not evolve, pivot, or reach for a new growth driver. The same flywheel is recited quarter after quarter with metronomic consistency — *organic double-digit growth in the Flight Support Group (FSG), bolt-on accretive acquisitions funded entirely by operating cash, and disciplined de-levering between deals.* In Q2-FY25 Victor Mendelson frames it as "strong 14% organic growth from increased demand across all of our product lines and the impact from our profitable fiscal 2025 and 2024 acquisitions." By Q4-FY25 the identical sentence structure appears with "strong 16% organic growth," and by Q1-FY26 "strong organic growth of 12%." The narrative is a **loop, not an arc** — and for a 36-year compounder that is the point. Management explicitly resists thesis-expansion: in Q3-FY25 Eric Mendelson rejects centralizing the supply chain for margin ("I don't think culturally... in the next 3 to 5 years"), and in Q1-FY26 he disavows price-grabs on mature PMA parts ("that's really not the HEICO way... I would not model that kind of activity"). The only genuinely new element is the **founder succession**: Larry Mendelson's death surfaces in the Q4-FY25 call (2025-12-19), handled as a values-transmission story ("That was his succession plan... It worked perfectly") rather than a discontinuity. Growth drivers introduced and sustained: defense/space (Golden Dome, "$1.5T budget"), Wencor cross-sell, and a relentless M&A cadence (5 deals in FY25, Gables the 3rd-largest in company history, plus Ethos/Axillon/Sherwood/Southwest Antennas into FY26).

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Where stated | Financial evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record" sales & operating income every single quarter | All 4 calls | Rev 1.87B→2.21B→2.97B→3.86B→4.49B (FY21–25); 3-yr CAGR +26.6% | **CORROBORATED** — the records are real, not framing |
| Double-digit FSG organic growth | 14%/13%/16%/12% across calls | Live Q2-FY26: FSG +19% organic, sales record $929.4M | **CORROBORATED and accelerating** |
| Margin leverage from SG&A discipline | Q3-FY25 ("revenues outpace SG&A") | Live Q2-FY26: FSG op margin 26.2% (from 24.1%), ETG 26.5% | **CORROBORATED** — the scan's "gross margin contracting" flag is STALE; consolidated margins are EXPANDING |
| Cash funds growth, debt stays low | Every call ("never incurred debt to grow organically") | Net debt/EBITDA 1.86x→1.90x→1.60x→1.79x→**1.74x** (Q2-FY26); deleveraged even after $630M of FY25 deals | **CORROBORATED** — disciplined, self-funding |
| EPS compounding | "record net income" repeated | EPS 2.21→2.55→2.91→3.67→4.90; TTM diluted 5.60; Q2-FY26 $1.66 vs $1.33 est (+25% beat) | **CORROBORATED** — beat 7/7 qtrs, now 8/8 |
| "Earnings are real / never a one-time charge" | Q1-FY26 ("never had a one-time unusual charge") | FCF margin 19.2%; TTM FCF $926M; OCF 130% of NI (Q3-FY25) | **CORROBORATED** — cash conversion is genuine |

The narrative is **poorer than the numbers in only one respect**: management is conservative to a fault, repeatedly *declining* available margin upside (PMA price resets, supply-chain centralization). The story is, if anything, *less* aggressive than the trajectory would justify — the opposite of the usual forensic red flag. The single place the *valuation* diverges from the story: ROIC reads 10.0% in the scan, which understates true returns (depressed by acquisition-goodwill accounting on Wencor/Gables) but is NOT a 30%+ compounder-quality return on a clean basis — the multiple (60x P/E) is doing heavy lifting that the reported returns alone don't support.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING (from an already-high base).** Confidence is uniformly, almost euphorically high and rises into FY26. Q2-FY25: Eric Mendelson — "Our results were absolutely phenomenal... hit the ball out of the park." Q4-FY25: "Strong would be an understatement, but 16% organic growth." Q1-FY26: "We remain very bullish and optimistic." There is **zero shift from offensive to defensive language** — the entire vocabulary is growth/investment/acquisition, never cost-cutting or macro-defense. Tariff and China-destocking questions (Q2-FY25, Q4-FY25) are answered with confident dismissal ("we see strength in all of our areas... I wouldn't say that we've seen that"). Guidance specificity is *deliberately low* — HEICO famously does not give numeric guidance — but the qualitative confidence is maximal and consistent. The one somber note (Larry Mendelson's passing) is handled as continuity, not vulnerability.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** HEICO provides no formal numeric guidance, so the test is whether the *qualitative* promises (continued double-digit organic growth, accretive M&A, de-levering) are delivered. They are, every quarter: the screener shows **beat 7/7 quarters (100%)**, now 8/8 after Q2-FY26's $1.66 vs $1.33 (+25%). There is a structural **sandbagging pattern** baked into the culture — Q1-FY26's monologue on "properly reserving for obsolete inventory... not swept under the rug to protect reported earnings" and "never had a one-time unusual charge" is a deliberate signal that reported earnings are *under*-stated relative to economic reality, not inflated. Acquisitions promised "accretive within the year" (Gables, Rosen, Exxelia comps) track to that. The credibility gap is the inverse of most names: the risk is not that management over-promises, but that the *market* has fully priced the reliability into a 60x multiple.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts probe four recurring areas, and management's answers hold up: **(1) Tariff/China demand** (Gavin Parsons UBS Q2-FY25; Gautam Khanna Q4-FY25) — management acknowledges a brief China order-pause "to send a message" but reports no durable hit; live Q2-FY26 confirms no demand crack. **(2) Margin upside from PMA pricing** (Kristine Liwag MS Q3-FY25) — management explicitly *refuses* to harvest it, a governance positive but a near-term margin ceiling the bulls can't model. **(3) Dual-class collapse / Class A discount** (Khanna Q4-FY25) — Victor calls Class A "a screaming value" but signals "status quo," no catalyst. **(4) Stock-comp / amortization drag** (Louis Raffetto, Wolfe, Q3-FY25 & Q1-FY26) — Carlos Macau flags elevated stock-comp front-loaded in FY26 from performance-vesting, tapering later; a modeling nuisance, not a thesis-breaker. **No deflected question later turned into bad news** across this window — a clean record.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **8/8 earnings beats; FSG organic growth accelerating to 19% (Q2-FY26)** — the flywheel is intact and speeding up.
🟢 **Margins EXPANDING** (FSG 24.1%→26.2%, ETG to 26.5%) — refutes the scan's stale "gross margin contracting" flag.
🟢 **Self-funding M&A, de-levering to 1.74x net-debt/EBITDA** even after a heavy deal year — pristine balance sheet, Altman-Z 8.4.
🟢 **Conservative-accounting culture** (reserving discipline, no one-time charges, declining easy margin grabs) — the rarest green flag: management *understating* economic earnings.
🔴 **Valuation, not operations, is the red flag** — 60x trailing / 63x fwd P/E, P/FCF 55x, **negative margin of safety on every model** (DCF −100%, EPV −100%, Graham −59%, Owner-Earnings −100%); price 7% above the screener's own Buffett fair value.
🔴 **Insider open-market activity skews to SELLING** — 21 buys (17,613 sh) vs 13 sells (230,496 sh); the real-conviction signal is net sales by volume, not the grant-inflated 0.08 buy_ratio.
🔴 **Founder-era succession just completed** (Larry Mendelson's death, Q4-FY25) — execution risk is low given the embedded culture, but the irreplaceable capital-allocator is gone.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

The most revealing subtext is the **deliberate refusal to optimize for the multiple**. Eric Mendelson, Q1-FY26: "We are not into programs of the year, buzzwords or comparing ourselves to others hoping to get a higher multiple on our shares." This is a tell that management knows the stock is richly valued and is signaling they will *not* financial-engineer to defend it — a long-term-owner posture that protects terminal value but offers no near-term re-rating lever. Second signal: the **Class A "screaming value"** remark (Q4-FY25) plus directors buying Class A — a quiet acknowledgment that the *voting-share premium* is the only obvious intra-capital-structure value, with no plan to close it. Third: the **CapEx discipline holding at ~1.5–1.6% of revenue** (Carlos Macau, Q4-FY25) — an asset-light, high-return model, but also confirms growth is being *bought* (M&A) rather than built, so the compounding depends on an unbroken acquisition pipeline at sane prices.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STRENGTHENING.** Every operating and balance-sheet vector is improving — accelerating organic growth, expanding margins, falling leverage, a healthy M&A pipeline, and an 8/8 beat record. This is a genuine best-in-class quality compounder.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *this is a price problem, not a business problem.* The business deserves a premium; the question is whether **60x trailing earnings on a high-single/low-double-digit forward EPS-growth consensus (+12.8%)** leaves any margin of safety, with every intrinsic-value model showing the stock 30–100% above fair value and the load-bearing "catalyst" (Q2-FY26 blowout) **already fired and already in the price** (the stock surged on the print, analysts uniformly raised targets to ~$386 avg). There is no special-situation asymmetry here — no spinoff, no activist, no dated binding event. The only path to alpha is paying ~60x and trusting the compounder to grow into it, which is a quality-momentum bet, not a mispricing.

**ECONOMIC MOAT.** Type: **regulatory-license + switching-cost + intangible (PMA franchise)** — FAA Parts Manufacturer Approval is a years-long, capital-and-relationship-intensive certification moat; airlines that qualify HEICO parts onto fleets face high re-qualification switching costs; the 30–70% discount-to-OEM pricing creates demand stickiness. **Width: WIDE.** **Trend: WIDENING** — the evidence is unambiguous: expanding operating margins (FSG to 26.2%, ETG to 26.5%), accelerating organic growth (to 19% in FSG), Wencor cross-sell driving DER/PMA mix-shift that *raises* profitability, and rising EPS/cash-flow CAGRs. The reported ROIC of 10% understates true economics (goodwill-laden from M&A); on an organic/incremental basis returns are far higher and rising.

**SECULAR THREAT: NONE.** No structural force impairs HEICO's terminal value. The opposite — secular aftermarket tailwinds (aging fleets, OEM-parts shortages, airlines seeking cost-effective PMA/DER alternatives, defense/space budget expansion). AI, fintech, autonomy, channel-shift are irrelevant to FAA-approved physical aircraft parts. The cheapness signal here is **absent** — this is not a fear-print (the stock is *expensive*) and not a value trap (the base is *growing*); it is a fully-priced quality compounder. The risk is multiple compression, not terminal-value erosion.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 5 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING | MOAT: WIDE | MOAT_TREND: WIDENING | SECULAR_THREAT: none
